Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like I had to, usually sprawled out on my bed or floor with my notes around me. Without internet at home, I couldn't just hop online whenever, so I'd try to download stuff at the library and then work through it later. Most weeks I'd put in maybe 16 hours, but it was scattered and unfocused. I learn better by doing things with my hands anyway, so reading screens never felt natural to me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly a deadline would loom and I'd scramble. I don't have regular internet access, so I couldn't just log in whenever I wanted. I'd have to plan trips to campus or the library, which made consistency really hard. It felt like I was always playing catch-up rather than staying ahead.
Q3: Usually it was because something was due soon, or I needed to check if there was an assignment I'd missed. Sometimes I'd get anxious wondering if I'd forgotten something important, and that would push me to find a computer. I wasn't logging in to explore or engage—it was more like damage control. The anxiety about missing deadlines was honestly the main driver.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me because accessing it was such a hassle without home internet. I'd go to the library maybe once a week, download what I could, and then disappear again. It always felt like an interruption to my actual studying rather than part of it. I never got into a flow with it.
Q5: The videos were somewhat useful when I could actually download them and watch offline. I liked being able to pause and rewind, work through things at my own pace. Discussion posts occasionally had helpful explanations from other students that made concepts click. But honestly, I found physical notes and hands-on practice more valuable than most of what was on the LMS.
Q6: I ignored most of the optional readings and extra resources. They felt like busywork, and with my limited access, I had to prioritize. I'd skim discussion forums but rarely contributed because by the time I got online, conversations had moved on. The interactive quizzes and things that required constant internet access were basically useless to me.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams. If it was something I could do offline or hands-on, even better. Abstract readings or discussions that seemed to go in circles just bored me. I needed to feel like I was actually accomplishing something tangible, not just checking boxes.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. I was already struggling to keep up with required work, so anything labeled optional went straight to the bottom of my list. Required work at least had consequences attached, which gave me a reason to push through the boredom. Optional stuff just felt like extra weight I couldn't afford to carry.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded PDFs, handwritten notes, and borrowed textbooks. I'd print things out at the library and work through them at home. I also learned a lot by just physically practicing concepts—drawing diagrams, making flashcards, walking through problems step by step. The LMS was just where I grabbed materials, not where I learned.
Q10: There were tons of times I was studying hard but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours with printed lecture notes, rewriting them by hand to understand them better. I'd work through practice problems on paper, not on any platform. My actual learning happened in my room with a pen and paper, not clicking through screens.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my whole approach. I'd download everything I could in one session—videos, PDFs, whatever—and then not log back in for days while I worked through it all. One video might take me a whole week to fully process because I'd watch it, take notes, practice, rewatch sections. But the LMS would just show one quick access.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I just couldn't bring myself to care. When my anxiety spiked, I'd freeze up and avoid everything course-related. Sometimes the boredom was so intense that opening any course material felt physically draining. Not having reliable internet made these moments worse because catching up felt impossible once I fell behind.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a big one—I'd be exhausted from other stuff and just couldn't muster the energy to trek to the library. Competing responsibilities like work and family things constantly pulled my attention away. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes just shut down instead of seeking help because the effort felt overwhelming.
Q14: Oh, all the time. I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things. I'd scroll through readings without absorbing anything, just going through the motions. My anxiety would have me logged in and technically 'active' but I was really just spiraling internally, not learning anything.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like this huge wall I couldn't climb. I knew they mattered, but the boredom and dread would combine into this paralysis. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuses. It's a terrible cycle I haven't figured out how to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and procrastinate indefinitely. When something was due soon, I'd finally force myself to the library and cram everything in. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only reliable motivator I had. My LMS activity probably spiked right before every due date.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. If it was positive, I'd feel briefly okay, but it didn't motivate me to do more. Negative feedback just fed my anxiety and made me want to avoid the course even more. It felt disconnected from my actual learning process.
Q18: The course structure felt very online-focused, which didn't work well for someone like me without reliable internet. Everything assumed you could just hop on whenever and participate in real-time discussions. The weekly modules were fine in theory, but I was always a step behind because of access issues. A more flexible structure would've helped me a lot.
Q19: The heavy reliance on online participation made everything harder for me. Discussion deadlines that required being online at specific times were nearly impossible to meet. On the other hand, having downloadable materials was helpful—I just wished there was more of that. More hands-on assignments or offline options would have suited my learning style better.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for something, trying to figure out what they need, but struggling to actually produce work. Maybe they're anxious like me and keep checking things without being able to commit to completing assignments. Or they're confused and hoping clicking around will eventually make things clear. High activity doesn't mean high understanding.
Q21: That sounds like me, honestly. I performed okay on exams because I studied a lot offline in ways the LMS couldn't track. I'd spend hours with physical materials, practicing and memorizing, but my login count stayed low. The LMS only sees one slice of what students do. Someone can be genuinely learning without constantly being online.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and for how long, which gives a basic picture of activity patterns. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether someone was actually thinking or just clicking. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or whether the material actually made sense. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not about how many times you log in or how long you watch videos. For me, engagement happens when I'm working through problems by hand, making connections, feeling curious instead of bored. It's internal, not measurable by clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I carry about this course, or how that anxiety sometimes freezes me completely. It wouldn't capture the hours I spend studying with printed materials at home. It can't see that I'm actually motivated to learn but struggling with access and mental blocks. There's a whole person behind those login numbers that the data just misses.
